title: Switching from PostgreSQL to ClickHouse for Improved Performance and Scalability
gist: Momentic, an AI-driven software-testing platform, migrated its cache from PostgreSQL to ClickHouse after the cache table exploded from ~80,000 to 1 billion entries, hitting lock contention and resource limits. The rearchitecture scaled to 20 billion entries and 2M+ queries/day at ~250ms average latency, and eliminated the Redis layer entirely.

insights.claim: Momentic's cache table grew from roughly 80,000 to 1 billion entries, exposing PostgreSQL's lock contention and elevated resource usage under a concurrent high-write, high-read workload.
insights.speaker: Sergio De Simone
insights.so_what: A concrete scale threshold where B-tree-indexed OLTP databases break down for cache workloads, informing when teams should switch engines.
insights.quote: we were running into both elevated resource usage as well as lock contention from queries trying to read and write to the cache concurrently

insights.claim: The final system scaled to 20 billion entries handling over 2 million queries per day at ~250ms average latency, up from ~600,000 lookups per day at migration time.
insights.speaker: Sergio De Simone
insights.so_what: Quantifies ClickHouse's headroom for cache-style workloads at multi-billion-row scale, a reference point for capacity planning.
insights.quote: Momentic was able to scale its cache to 20 billion entries, handling over two million queries per day
insights.locator: As a result of this rearchitecture

insights.claim: ClickHouse's sparse primary indexes narrow searches to a small number of 'granules' when key values are known, whereas PostgreSQL query cost grows with data size, giving ClickHouse a structural efficiency advantage at scale.
insights.speaker: Sergio De Simone
insights.so_what: The core architectural reason column stores beat row stores for known-key lookups at scale—a durable database-selection principle.
insights.quote: ClickHouse can narrow searches to a small number of “granules” when key values are known

insights.claim: A carefully designed primary key (test ID, step ID, Momentic version, git branch, commit timestamp) achieved efficient lookups in 90% of cases, but main-branch queries read nearly all data parts, causing spiky memory and disk usage until materialized views precomputed commit timestamps.
insights.speaker: Sergio De Simone
insights.so_what: Shows primary-key design and materialized views as the key levers for taming outlier queries in ClickHouse, a practical migration lesson.
insights.quote: some outliers were reading almost all of the parts on every query, leading to spiky memory usage and disk operations
insights.locator: By carefully designing its primary key

insights.claim: Switching from three separate PostgreSQL queries to INSERT-only operations with ClickHouse's ReplacingMergeTree (letting the DB deduplicate asynchronously) improved performance enough to fully eliminate the Redis layer.
insights.speaker: Sergio De Simone
insights.so_what: A case where the right database engine removes an entire caching tier, cutting operational cost and complexity—negative signal for standalone cache layers at high key cardinality.
insights.quote: This was such an improvement that we were able to fully eliminate the Redis layer
insights.locator: Instead we switched to using only INSERTS

insights.claim: Momentic ran ClickHouse alongside PostgreSQL with dual writes, shadow-queried and diffed both databases for correctness, gradually shifted traffic, and kept dual writes after cutover to enable safe rollback.
insights.speaker: Sergio De Simone
insights.so_what: A reusable, low-risk database-migration pattern (dual-write, shadow-read, diff, gradual cutover) that de-risks major infra swaps.
insights.quote: results from both databases were diffed to ensure correctness
